question: Does the single control room concept (Ch.01) present regulatory concerns given the RCS complexity described in Ch.05?

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is responsible for removing heat from the reactor core and transferring it to the steam generators using natural circulation, driven by density and elevation differences. It includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated valves, and interfaces with several other safety and control systems to maintain pressure boundary integrity and facilitate the removal of noncondensible gases during both normal and emergency operations.

nuscale_ch01_0074.title: Conformance with Regulatory Criteria
nuscale_ch01_0074.summary: This section outlines how the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report demonstrates compliance with applicable regulatory criteria, using a series of tables to assess conformance with various NRC regulations and guidance documents effective six months prior to the anticipated docket date. It details the evaluation process, including identification of deviations and justifications for alternative approaches, and specifies that future applicants referencing the NuScale design must similarly address regulatory conformance for site-specific and operational aspects.

nuscale_ch01_0121.title: 1.9 Conformance with Regulatory Criteria
nuscale_ch01_0121.summary: This section outlines how the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report demonstrates compliance with applicable regulatory criteria, using a series of tables to assess conformance with various NRC regulations and guidance documents in effect six months prior to the anticipated docket date. It also specifies that any deviations from regulatory guidance are identified and justified, and that future applicants referencing the NuScale design must similarly address conformance for site-specific and operational aspects.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section describes the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS), which uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to ensure safe and efficient cooling, maintain proper water chemistry, and provide uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0370.title: SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3: Control Room Area Ventilation SystemII.2 Environmental and Dynamic Effects
nuscale_ch01_0370.summary: The section confirms that the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report meets the requirements of SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3, specifically regarding environmental and dynamic effects on the Control Room Area Ventilation System. It states that the design conforms to regulatory criteria, with no exceptions noted for this aspect.

nuscale_ch01_0372.title: SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3: Control Room Area Ventilation SystemII.4 Control Room
nuscale_ch01_0372.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3, regarding the Control Room Area Ventilation System. It confirms that the NuScale design meets regulatory criteria for protection against natural phenomena, environmental effects, and control room sharing, and clarifies that the control room habitability system uses compressed air tanks for clean air rather than emergency filtration during accident conditions. The table also notes that certain criteria are not applicable or are met except for aspects related to ESF atmosphere cleanup systems.

nuscale_ch01_0851.title: SRP 18.0, Rev 2: Human Factors EngineeringII.C Review of the HFE Aspects of Modifications Affecting Risk Important Human Actions
nuscale_ch01_0851.summary: This section states that the SRP 18.0, Rev 2 criteria for reviewing human factors engineering (HFE) aspects of modifications affecting risk-important human actions are not applicable to NuScale, as they pertain to existing reactor licensees. However, the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report conforms to the review criteria in SRP Appendix 18-A for crediting manual operator actions in diversity and defense-in-depth analyses, while site-specific hazards and PRA updates are the responsibility of the COL applicant.

nuscale_ch01_0066.title: Material Referenced
nuscale_ch01_0066.summary: This section lists the topical and technical reports referenced in the NuScale Power Plant Design Certification Application, as detailed in Tables 1.6-1 and 1.6-2. These reports cover various aspects such as quality assurance, risk significance, safety classification, accident analysis, and methodology qualifications, and are incorporated by reference into the Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR).

nuscale_ch01_0050.title: Diesel Generator Buildings
nuscale_ch01_0050.summary: The Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs) in the NuScale Power Plant each house a single backup diesel generator and their auxiliary equipment, but do not contain any safety-related systems or support engineered safety functions (ESFs). Their primary purpose is to provide support and housing for the generators, associated equipment, HVAC system, and maintenance area.

nuscale_ch01_0049.title: Central Utility Building
nuscale_ch01_0049.summary: The Central Utility Building (CUB) is a non-safety-related structure that houses essential utility services for the plant, such as chiller equipment, air systems, demineralized water equipment, and security functions. It provides spaces for monitoring access and security, but does not contain safety-related systems. The plant design also emphasizes minimizing human error through automation, digital controls, and effective workload management, as detailed in the Human Factors Engineering (HFE) program.

nuscale_ch01_0111.title: Figure 1.7-2: Instrumentatio n and Controls Symbol Legend
nuscale_ch01_0111.summary: This section provides a legend explaining the symbols used for instrumentation and controls in NuScale’s safety analysis diagrams. It defines the logic gate functions, control room indicators, switch types, and other symbols relevant to system status and operations, ensuring clear interpretation of the diagrams. Additionally, it includes notes on piping and instrumentation diagram conventions, such as vent and drain representations.

nuscale_ch01_0402.title: DSRS 9.5.2, Rev 0: Communication SystemsII.11 Compliance with GDC 19
nuscale_ch01_0402.summary: This section explains that the NuScale design departs from GDC 19 by supporting an exemption and instead complies with a NuScale-specific principal design criterion (PDC-19). The design ensures that communication systems in the control room can support all normal and emergency operations, including intra-plant and off-site communications, even during single failures or power loss. Additionally, certain regulatory requirements (10 CFR 73.45) are not applicable to NuScale, as it does not reprocess spent fuel or handle special nuclear material.

nuscale_ch01_0072.title: Combined License Information Items
nuscale_ch01_0072.summary: This section explains the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant certified design and the site-specific design provided in the combined license (COL) application. It distinguishes between conceptual design information (CDI) for non-certified portions and COL information items, which are site-specific responsibilities. The section also notes that COL applicants must address all COL information items and provide a list of any departures from the certified design.

nuscale_ch01_0687.title: DSRS 15.1.5, Rev 0: Steam System Piping Failures Inside and Outside of ContainmentII.9 AssumptionsPostulated Failure of Seismic Main Steam Line
nuscale_ch01_0687.summary: This section outlines the assumptions related to postulated failures of both seismic and non-seismic main steam lines in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, confirming conformance with DSRS 15.1.5 requirements. It notes that operator action is not required to mitigate steam line break consequences, and clarifies that radiological consequence analyses are governed by SRP Section 15.0.3, while nonradiological aspects remain under DSRS 15.1.5.

nuscale_ch01_0853.title: SRP Appendix 18-A, Rev 0: Crediting Manual Operator Actions in Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) AnalysesC.2.B Review Criteria for Phase 2 (Preliminary Validation)
nuscale_ch01_0853.summary: This section summarizes NuScale's conformance with regulatory criteria regarding the crediting of manual operator actions in Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) analyses, as outlined in SRP Appendix 18-A, Rev 0. It confirms that NuScale meets the review criteria for all phases (analysis, preliminary validation, integrated system validation, and long-term integrity), superseding previous guidance, while noting that certain aspects of probabilistic risk assessment remain the responsibility of the COL applicant.

nuscale_ch01_0852.title: SRP Appendix 18-A, Rev 0: Crediting Manual Operator Actions in Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) AnalysesC.1.B Review Criteria for Phase 1 (Analysis)
nuscale_ch01_0852.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for crediting manual operator actions in Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) analyses, as outlined in SRP Appendix 18-A, Rev 0. It confirms that NuScale meets the review criteria for all phases of D3 analysis, superseding previous guidance, while noting that certain aspects of probabilistic risk assessment (PRA) remain the responsibility of the COL applicant.

nuscale_ch01_0662.title: DSRS 15.0.3, Rev 0: Design Basis Accident Radiological Consequence Analyses for the NuScale SMR DesignII.2 Control Room Radiological Habitability
nuscale_ch01_0662.summary: This section summarizes the NuScale SMR’s compliance with regulatory criteria for radiological consequence analyses during design basis accidents, specifically addressing control room radiological habitability. It confirms that the NuScale design meets the relevant requirements of DSRS 15.0.3, with no exceptions noted for control room habitability, ensuring the safety of personnel during postulated accident scenarios. The section also references conformance tables aligning the design with NUREG-0800 and DSRS standards.

nuscale_ch01_0004.title: Containment Type
nuscale_ch01_0004.summary: The NuScale containment vessel is a cylindrical, steel, ASME-certified pressure vessel designed to withstand high-pressure events and operates under vacuum during normal conditions, eliminating the need for insulation. The reactor is a passive, integral small modular pressurized water reactor (SMR) with natural circulation and no external piping for steam generators or pressurizer, and each module produces about 50 MWe, with a 12-module plant yielding approximately 570 MWe net output.

nuscale_ch01_0663.title: DSRS 15.0.3, Rev 0: Design Basis Accident Radiological Consequence Analyses for the NuScale SMR DesignII.3 Technical Support Center Radiological Habitability
nuscale_ch01_0663.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for radiological consequence analyses during design basis accidents, specifically addressing the Technical Support Center (TSC) radiological habitability. It notes that the TSC meets dose acceptance criteria when AC power is available, but its function is transferred to the main control room if AC power is lost, indicating only partial conformance for TSC habitability.

nuscale_ch01_0003.title: Plant Location
nuscale_ch01_0003.summary: The section introduces the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), which is required for standard design certification under U.S. regulations. It outlines the modular, scalable design of the NuScale Power Module (NPM), highlighting key safety features such as passive shutdown and cooling, compact containment, and advanced digital controls, with the plant capable of operating between one and twelve modules within a single facility.

nuscale_ch01_0164.title: SRP 6.4, Rev 3: Control Room Habitability SystemII.7 Toxic Gas Hazards
nuscale_ch01_0164.summary: The NuScale control room habitability system does not rely on emergency ventilation but instead uses compressed air tanks to ensure clean air during accidents, eliminating the risk of toxic gas or radioactive material entering via ventilation. The guidance on toxic gas hazards is only partially applicable, as programmatic requirements fall under the responsibility of the COL applicant. Additionally, NuScale does not use engineered safety feature (ESF) filter or ventilation systems for accident mitigation, and such systems are not credited in their dose analysis.

nuscale_ch01_0161.title: SRP 6.4, Rev 3: Control Room Habitability SystemII.5 Relative Location of Source and Control Room
nuscale_ch01_0161.summary: This section explains that certain regulatory criteria related to control room habitability and emergency ventilation systems are not applicable to the NuScale design, as it uses compressed air tanks to maintain control room air quality during accidents, eliminating reliance on ventilation inlets. Dose guidelines for new reactors are partially applicable, while toxic gas hazard requirements are the responsibility of the COL applicant. Additionally, NuScale does not use engineered safety feature (ESF) filter or ventilation systems for accident mitigation, so related requirements do not apply.
